name: The Game (Easy Version)
statement:
v>
This is the easy version of the problem. The difference between the versions is that in this version, you only need to find one of the possible nodes Cirno may choose. You can hack only if you solved all versions of this problem.
Cirno and Daiyousei are playing a game with a tree$^{\text{Ôêù}}$ of $n$ nodes, rooted at node $1$. The value of the $i$-th node is $w_i$. They take turns to play the game; Cirno goes first.
In each turn, assuming the opponent chooses $j$ in the last turn, the player can choose any remaining node $i$ satisfying $w_i \gt w_j$ and delete the subtree$^{\text{ÔÇá}}$ of node $i$. In particular, Cirno can choose any node and delete its subtree in the first turn.
The first player who can not operate wins, and they all hope to win. Find one of the possible nodes Cirno may choose so that she wins if both of them play optimally.
$^{\text{Ôêù}}$A tree is a connected graph without cycles.
$^{\text{ÔÇá}}$Node $u$ is considered in the subtree of node $i$ if any path from $1$ to $u$ must go through $i$.
input:
The first line of input contains a single integer $t$ ($1 \leq t \leq 10^5$) ÔÇö the number of input test cases.
The first line of each test case contains one integer $n$ ($1 \le n \le 4\cdot 10^5$) ÔÇö the number of nodes in the tree.
The second line contains $n$ integers $w_1,w_2,\ldots,w_n$ ($1 \le w_i \le n$) ÔÇö the value of each node.
The next $n-1$ lines contain the edges of the tree. The $i$-th line contains two integers $u_i,v_i$ ($1\le u_i,v_i \le n$, $u_i \neq v_i$) ÔÇö an edge connecting $u_i$ and $v_i$. It is guaranteed that the given edges form a tree.
It is guaranteed that the sum of $n$ over all test cases does not exceed $4\cdot 10^5$.
output:
For each test case, print one line.
If Cirno wins the game, print any possible node she may choose in the first turn.
Otherwise, print "0" (without quotes).
note:
In the first test case:
ÔÇó  If Cirno chooses $1$ or $3$ in the first turn, Daiyousei cannot make a move, so Daiyousei wins.
ÔÇó  If Cirno chooses $2$ or $4$ in the first turn, Daiyousei can only choose $3$, but after it Cirno cannot make a move, so Cirno wins.
Therefore, all possible nodes Cirno may choose are $2$ and $4$.
In the second test case, regardless of which node Cirno chooses, Daiyousei cannot make a move, so Daiyousei wins.
In the third and fourth test case, the only possible node Cirno may choose in the first turn is $2$.
In the fifth test case, all possible nodes Cirno may choose in the first turn are $3,4,6,7$ and $10$.
